examples.title: Eliminate ε-Productions
examples.description: Algorithm to remove ε-productions from a CFG
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RemoveEpsilonProductions
BEGIN
  // Step 1: Find nullable variables
  Nullable ← ∅
  REPEAT
    For each production A → α
      IF α = ε THEN Nullable ← Nullable ∪ {A}
      ELSE IF all symbols in α are in Nullable
           THEN Nullable ← Nullable ∪ {A}
  UNTIL no new variables added to Nullable

  // Step 2: Generate new productions
  NewProductions ← ∅
  For each production A → X1X2...Xk
    For each subset of nullable variables in RHS
      Generate production without that subset
      Add to NewProductions
  
  // Step 3: Add S → ε if S is nullable
  IF S ∈ Nullable AND S → ε ∉ Productions
    Add S → ε to Productions
  
  Remove all ε-productions from Productions
  Add NewProductions to Productions
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm first identifies all nullable variables (those that can derive ε), then creates new productions by removing nullable variables in all possible combinations from right-hand sides, and finally removes original ε-productions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × 2^m) where n is productions, m is nullable variables

examples.title: Eliminate Unit Productions
examples.description: Algorithm to remove unit productions (A → B) from a CFG
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RemoveUnitProductions
BEGIN
  // Step 1: Compute unit closure for each variable
  For each variable A
    UnitClosure[A] ← {A}
    Repeat
      For each unit production A → B in UnitClosure[A]
        Add all productions B → α to UnitClosure[A]
    Until no new productions added

  // Step 2: Create new non-unit productions
  NewProductions ← ∅
  For each variable A
    For each B in UnitClosure[A]
      For each non-unit production B → α
        Add A → α to NewProductions

  // Step 3: Replace productions
  Remove all unit productions from Productions
  Add NewProductions to Productions
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm computes the transitive closure of unit productions for each variable, then adds all non-unit productions that can be reached through unit chains directly to the starting variable.

examples.title: CYK Algorithm Implementation
examples.description: Python code for CYK membership algorithm requiring CNF grammar
examples.explanation: The CYK algorithm uses dynamic programming to check if a string can be derived from the start symbol. It builds a table where P[i][j] contains variables that can derive the substring from position i to j. Works by trying all possible splits and checking if both halves can be derived by variables in CNF productions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n³ × |P|) where n is string length, |P| is number of productions

examples.title: Convert to CNF Form
examples.description: C code showing conversion of terminal+variable productions to CNF
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the CNF conversion process: first, convert all terminals appearing with variables to new variables (terminals must appear alone or with only variables in CNF). Second, split productions with more than 2 variables on RHS into binary productions using new intermediate variables.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n is number of productions, m is max RHS length
